entries.title: Pont du Gard, France
entries.description: Built by the Romans around 50 AD, this aqueduct bridge stands three tiers high and stretches 900 feet across the Gardon River. It remains the tallest surviving Roman aqueduct in the world.

entries.title: Covered Bridge at Lanterman's Mill, Ohio
entries.description: Nestled in Youngstown's Mill Creek Park, this 1870 covered bridge shelters travelers above a rushing millrace. Its hand-hewn timber joints have endured floods, ice storms, and more than 150 winters.

entries.title: Brooklyn Bridge, New York
entries.description: When it opened in 1883, the Brooklyn Bridge was the longest suspension bridge in the world. Chief Engineer John Roebling's Gothic granite towers still define the New York skyline 140 years later.

entries.title: Natural Bridge, Virginia
entries.description: Carved by Cedar Creek over millennia, this 215-foot-tall limestone arch once carried the main road through the Shenandoah Valley. George Washington reportedly surveyed it and carved his initials into its stone wall.

entries.title: Iron Bridge, Ironbridge, England
entries.description: Completed in 1781, the Iron Bridge over the River Severn was the world's first cast iron bridge. Its construction marked a pivotal moment in the Industrial Revolution and gave the surrounding town its name.

entries.title: Hartland Covered Bridge, New Brunswick
entries.description: At 1,282 feet, this 1901 structure is the world's longest covered wooden bridge. The roof and sides were added in 1920 — according to local custom, couples who kiss beneath its rafters are bound for a long marriage.

entries.title: Kinzua Viaduct, Pennsylvania
entries.description: Built in 1882 and soaring 301 feet above Kinzua Creek, this iron railroad viaduct was once called the Eighth Wonder of the World. Five of its towers were toppled by a tornado in 2003 and now stand as a monument to nature's power.
